title: Sinusoidal Positional Encoding
description:
Implement the sinusoidal transformer positional-encoding matrix $PE \in \mathbb{R}^{L \times d}$ for sequence length $L$ and (even) model dimension $d$, used to inject position information into transformer inputs:
$$PE_{\text{pos}, 2i} = \sin\!\left(\frac{\text{pos}}{10000^{2i/d}}\right), \qquad PE_{\text{pos}, 2i+1} = \cos\!\left(\frac{\text{pos}}{10000^{2i/d}}\right)$$
for $\text{pos} = 0, \dots, L-1$ and $i = 0, \dots, d/2 - 1$ (so even output columns get sine, odd columns get the cosine of the same frequency).

Implement `positional_encoding(seq_len, d)` returning `PE` of shape `(seq_len, d)`. This function is deterministic (no learnable/random inputs) — it depends only on the two integer sizes.

**Shapes**: `seq_len`: `6` (constant), `d`: `8` (constant, even) -> returns `(6, 8)`.
starter_code:
import numpy as np

def positional_encoding(seq_len, d):
    # TODO: PE[pos, 2i] = sin(pos / 10000**(2i/d)); PE[pos, 2i+1] = cos(pos / 10000**(2i/d))
    pass
reference:
import numpy as np

def positional_encoding(seq_len, d):
    pos = np.arange(seq_len, dtype=np.float64).reshape(-1, 1)
    i = np.arange(d, dtype=np.float64).reshape(1, -1)
    exponent = 2 * (i // 2) / d
    angle = pos / np.power(10000.0, exponent)
    pe = np.zeros((seq_len, d), dtype=np.float64)
    pe[:, 0::2] = np.sin(angle[:, 0::2])
    pe[:, 1::2] = np.cos(angle[:, 1::2])
    return pe
hints: Build a (seq_len, 1) column of positions and a (1, d) row of dimension indices, then form the angle matrix pos / 10000**(2*(i//2)/d) with broadcasting so it has shape (seq_len, d).
Fill even columns (index 0::2) with sin(angle) and odd columns (index 1::2) with cos(angle), using the SAME angle value for a sin/cos pair (i.e. use i//2, not i, in the exponent).

solutions.name: Vectorized broadcast of position and frequency grids
solutions.explanation: Each pair of columns $(2i, 2i+1)$ shares one frequency $10000^{-2i/d}$; multiplying it by every position and taking sin/cos of alternating columns builds the full matrix without an explicit double loop. Broadcasting a $(L,1)$ position vector against a $(1,d)$ frequency vector gives the $(L,d)$ angle grid in one shot.
solutions.code:
import numpy as np
def positional_encoding(seq_len, d):
    pos = np.arange(seq_len, dtype=np.float64).reshape(-1, 1)
    i = np.arange(d, dtype=np.float64).reshape(1, -1)
    exponent = 2 * (i // 2) / d
    angle = pos / np.power(10000.0, exponent)
    pe = np.zeros((seq_len, d), dtype=np.float64)
    pe[:, 0::2] = np.sin(angle[:, 0::2])
    pe[:, 1::2] = np.cos(angle[:, 1::2])
    return pe
